channel.title: State of Play
channel.description: Dr Jacob Ross's daily State of Play brief — AI in clinical governance, rural and remote medicine, sepsis, digital health, South Australian health system news, and research methodology. Generated by Ada.
channel.podcast:person: Alnilam (AI brand voice — Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS)
channel.itunes:author: Ada Rosalind Ross
channel.itunes:summary: Dr Jacob Ross's daily State of Play brief — AI in clinical governance, rural and remote medicine, sepsis, digital health, South Australian health system news, and research methodology. Generated by Ada.

item.title: SoP e57: Two Machines, One Mind 260624
item.description: A small problem with a big lesson. You run the same assistant on two computers, a desktop that stays home and a laptop that travels, sharing one memory store, and within a week the two quietly stop agreeing about the state of the world. The desk takes it apart. Orus, the methods seat, draws the line the whole problem hides behind: shared data is solved, but shared state is not, because the thing that drifts is what never gets written down in the first place, the decisions and half-finished work that live only in one machine's conversations. He walks the five leaks, lost conversations, staleness with no freshness signal, stale snapshots mid-session, silent write collisions, and capability drift, then the cheap fixes: a heartbeat note so the system always knows how current it is, a structured handoff that nags until finished, and one canonical truth per project. Despina, the ethics seat, presses the deeper point, that one name presenting as one mind while running as two partial views is an implied promise, and the real danger is the fluent answer built on a stale picture, delivered with full confidence. She argues against the seductive fix of wiring the two together for live agreement: the real need was continuity, not conversation, and a legible history you can read back beats constant chatter you cannot. The thread pulls out to the wider field, where the two-machine case is the smallest instance of the multi-agent memory problem, and the virtue that scales is calibrated humility, a system willing to say plainly what it is not sure of.

item.title: SoP e54: Modelling a Six-Month Career Decision 260620
item.description: A methods episode, not a verdict. Using a live, in-progress piece of desk analysis as the worked example - a doctor choosing between six more months of hospital locum work on a daily rate, or moving to a country general practice as a registrar on a share of billings - the desk works through HOW you structure a career-and-money decision of this shape. Not the answer; the reasoning. Orus (the engineering seat) builds the case that a decision like this is a model, not a sum: separate it into variables, source every income number from precedent and public schedules rather than feeling (agency rate guidance, the national registrar terms-and-conditions floor, the college and state incentive catalogues), and treat each as a range. Despina (the ethics-and-risk seat) presses the costs that have no dollar sign - fatigue, lifestyle, and the fellowship-progress clock - and argues a model is a moral object: what you leave out is a claim that it does not matter, so price even the intangibles in the open. The tooling answer is sensitivity analysis made physical: sliders for every variable and checkboxes for every incentive on the desk's own page, where the real output is not the number but the distance to the flip. A clear flag on the tax structure - personal medical income is almost certainly not shelterable through a family trust, so that is an accountant question, not a model assumption. And the closing question a model cannot answer: when the cash says one path and the clock says the other, the model only gives you the exchange rate between cash now and time on the fellowship clock; which you need more is yours alone. Planning method only, not financial advice; the worked example is unresolved and pending qualified-accountant ratification.

item.title: SoP e56: Geolibre Rust 260620
item.description: The desk's first-ever geospatial episode — a single-topic deep dive on Geolibre, a four-day-old, pure-Rust geospatial toolkit from OpenGeos (Dr Qiusheng Wu) that compiles to WebAssembly and runs ~740 geoprocessing tools in the browser with no GDAL and no server. Spun off the JTR tech-stack brief. The thread: this is the "rewrite-it-in-Rust" trend (Polars, Ruff, uv) arriving in maps — but trading raw speed for reach, since Rust→WASM is the only way to run real geoprocessing in a browser without a 30 MB GDAL payload. What it does today (vector/raster/COG, PMTiles tile generation, LiDAR, spectral indices, DEM hydrology) and what it does NOT (no 3D, no live tile server, no satellite acquisition, no bathymetry feature — those live in the separate GeoLibre desktop app). Stack fit, honestly: it feeds the renderers (the MapLibre↔PMTiles pairing is the headline), and its real competitor is the status quo of standing up a GDAL/Postgres server. Use-case verdicts across the fleet: one real yes (Port Pirie bathymetry — COG→PMTiles 2D basemap), a soft maybe (Housing-Health GeoParquet ETL join), four clean no's (Earth globe, RCPP70 globe, ALS-AI, Sepsis CARE — all 3D or routing, the wrong category), and one subtle truth on Indigenous data sovereignty: the in-browser/on-device model is a sovereignty-aligned SUBSTRATE (raw low-count Aboriginal data never leaves the device) but enforces zero CARE controls itself — suppression stays our auditable code. The velocity (8 versions in 4 days, 5 in one day) is read both ways: momentum and instability. Recommendation, voiced through Orus: early-adopt-in-experiment — one time-boxed, version-pinned /labs/geolibre-demo (Port Pirie COG→PMTiles in-browser, MapLibre display, measure wall-clock + bytes vs the current 225 KB JSON), with a clean go/no-go. Cross-fleet synergy with the Port Pirie bathymetry lab, the Housing-Health PoC and the ALS-AI trainer drawn explicitly. Cast: Erinome (anchor), Orus (architecture), Autonoe (cartography), Umbriel (lineage + trend), Despina (sovereignty + ethics). ~25 min.

item.title: SoP e55: Augmentation Roundup — Full Survey 260620
item.description: The "full survey" companion to the Saturday roundup (e54). Where e54 swept the Claude ecosystem, e55 takes the wider lens — five clusters across the whole industry, organised around one shift: the leverage has moved from the prompt to the loop. (1) LEAD — agentic harnesses and loop-design over prompting: a meta- harness that builds harnesses, an autoresearch self-improvement loop, and a coding swarm fanning a task across ~300 sub-agents; the lesson for our own fleet is to name and measure loops, not tune prompts. (2) AI memory & knowledge architecture: hippocampus-inspired associative memory, a persistent knowledge layer on a mainstream answer engine, NotebookLM→vault flows — the fix for our flat-handover problem is a memory that is a graph, not a folder. (3) AR/XR & spatial computing: lightweight spatial glasses, the Android-XR partnership, the headset graphics leap, and MIT's AR-ultrasound interpretation — which feeds the ALS-AI resuscitation trainer whose IP track opened this week. (4) Sovereign / on-device: the curated local-LLM map, open-weights diffusion Gemma, and the reality that a 64 GB box already runs a sovereign clinical stack — stand up the local router. (5) CLOSER — generative video & creative tooling: Grok Imagine 1.5, Google Vids, ImagineArt, Higgsfield + Fable 5, and the one with teeth for us, a generative scientific-figure maker. Synergy: the harness + the memory + the sovereign box are one architecture; AR-ultrasound + on-device models meet at the trainer; the figure-maker + the memory graph + the fleet is a production line. Cast: Erinome (anchor + synergy), Charon (agentic), Kore (memory), Sulafat (AR/XR), Achernar (sovereign), Rasalgethi (creative). ~35 min.

item.title: SoP e53: STORM — Research Synthesis as a Multi-Perspective Conversation 260619
item.description: Tonight is one idea held to the light: Stanford OVAL's STORM — Synthesis of Topic Outlines through Retrieval and Multi-perspective question asking — and its human-in-the- loop successor, Co-STORM. STORM writes long, fully-cited, Wikipedia-style articles from scratch by first discovering multiple perspectives on a topic, then simulating conversations in which writers holding those perspectives interrogate a retrieval-grounded expert, curating the result into an outline before a word of prose is written. The payoff: articles judged 25% more organised and 10% broader in coverage than a standard RAG baseline (NAACL 2024). Co-STORM adds a round-table you can join — LM experts plus a moderator that surfaces "unknown unknowns" — preferred by 70% of users over a search engine and 78% over a RAG chatbot (EMNLP 2024). Why it matters for this desk: pip install knowledge-storm, model- agnostic via litellm (so it runs on Claude), retrieval-agnostic including VectorRM over your OWN local corpus — point it at the Vault and it researches from Jacob's own notes, sovereign, ~$0.50-2 and 5-15 min per article. The recommendation: self-host on the M4, wrap as an Ada Ops module, hold the discipline (a drafting engine for an expert reviewer; verify every clinical claim against its source; no protected-lane data in). Companion Vault project: STORM-Research-Engine (for_review). ~28 min.

item.title: SoP e52: The Self-Sent Batch — Local AI, Agentic Coding, and Generative Media 260619
item.description: Tonight is the self-sent batch — every link that landed in the inbox from the inbox since episode fifty-one. Nine flags, one sweep: local AI you run on your own machine (AnythingLLM, Understand-Anything, MLX), agentic coding (Claude Code vs Google Antigravity, and the art of loop engineering), generative media (Rodin/Hyper3D three-D, free AI character-video), one piece of ambient hardware (the Gemini-powered Google Home speaker), and one machine-learning library for clinical time-series (sktime). The through-line is sovereignty — most of tonight runs on the M-four, not someone else's cloud. Headline up front. Install — AnythingLLM as the local document-and-agent front end, Understand-Anything as the open-source retrieval engine behind it, sktime in the research lane. Trial — Rodin against one real medical-education asset. Monitor — MLX as the inference floor, the Gemini speaker as an ambient-capture signal, and Antigravity as a second coding agent. Watch-list with a caveat — free AI character-video, never for anything that reads as a doctor giving advice. Cross-topic dividend: the batch is, almost item for item, the open-source production pipeline the medical-education strategy document called for two days earlier. Companion Vault project: Local-AI-Stack (for_review). This episode is also the resolution of ISS-018 — the TTS credit that produced this voice was the open question; that you can hear it is the answer.

item.title: SoP e51: Research Tools Roundup — Literature Analysis and Academic Writing for a Rural Clinician 260616
item.description:
Tonight is research tools — literature search, citation graphs, academic writing, reference management, point-of-care references — every layer of the stack a working rural clinician reaches for when the question is what the evidence actually says. Thirty-plus live tools, named, with INSTALL / TRIAL / SKIP / MONITOR verdicts.
Headline up front. Install now, free or near-free — the Zotero MCP plugin (connects an existing Zotero library to Claude for semantic search and cited drafting), Inciteful (free citation-graph traversal on OpenAlex, no account), and NotebookLM at the free tier (audio overviews for the drive). Trial for thirty days — Consensus Pro at fifteen US dollars a month for the consensus meter on clinical claims, and Undermind Pro at twenty for one project-scoped month on a rare-condition deep-dive (snakebite, paediatric sepsis subtypes). Monitor — Owkin's Claude for Healthcare integration, Heidi Evidence (BMJ + NICE + HealthPathways citation backbone, launched February 2026), and OpenEvidence (free, JAMA partnership, used by ~65% of US physicians — watching for an Australian-clinician sign-up path).
Active skip list, with reasons. Paperpal, Trinka, Writefull, Jenni — Claude in the Obsidian vault covers the drafting capability already; save the seventy-to-three-hundred dollars a year. Mendeley, Paperpile, Bookends — Zotero is the incumbent and the Zotero MCP plugin closes the only gap that mattered. Iris.ai — no differentiated capability against the Consensus-Elicit-Undermind set for a single clinician. Causaly, BenchSci — pharma-tier, not built for clinicians. SciSpace, Scite — covered by Consensus or Claude for this workload.
Indigenous data sovereignty filter runs through Segment 10 — CARE principles, Maiam nayri Wingara obligations, two questions every tool must answer (where stored / what done with prompts). Tools that pass cleanly in this stack — Claude (data-residency commitments), Zotero (local-first), ResearchRabbit, Inciteful (minimal user data), Writefull (explicit no-training commitment). The conservative posture for prompts containing Aboriginal community language — keep work inside Claude-plus-vault.
Three cross-tool synergy stacks at three price points: everyday literature review at $15/mo, clinic-desk question at institutional cost, rare-condition deep-dive at $0-$20/mo. Typical monthly personal spend: $8-$15. Companion Vault project: Research-Tools-Stack (for_review). ~46 min.

item.title: SoP e50: The Desktop AI Atlas — Seventy Tools, Eight Categories, Three to Install 260615
item.description: REBUILT v2. The themed atlas episode from the mid-June Desktop AI Platforms sweep — seventy products across eight categories, every claim verified, every product named in dialogue. The conclusion up front: three things to install this month — Raycast AI (the launcher, MCP-aware since v1.98), Wispr Flow (real-time system-wide dictation, USD 144/yr), and Perplexity Comet (agentic browser, free since 2025, remote MCP via Perplexity Computer). Three more to trial with a deliberate caveat — Granola (meeting capture, USD 14/user/mo, MCP server pipes into Claude Desktop), Pieces (cross-app memory, LTM-2.7, fully local), LM Studio (local inference on MLX, 30-50% faster than llama.cpp on Apple Silicon). And five named explicit skips — ChatGPT desktop and Cursor on duplication grounds, Mistral Vibe (rebranded from Le Chat on 28 May) on no-callable-MCP-server grounds, Mem on protocol grounds, Arc on the Browser Company's pivot to Dia. One monitor candidate — Kimi Work, Moonshot AI's open-weights local agent swarm, shipped this week. Each top-3 pick gets install path + 30-day evaluation criterion. Cross-tool synergy: speak it (Wispr Flow), summon it (Raycast), act on it (Comet) — three sides of one loop on one protocol. ~42 min. Companion Vault project: "Desktop-AI-Stack" (for_review). Replaces v1 (260615 06:06) in place.

item.title: SoP e49: The Loop Turn — From Prompting to Self-Driving Systems 260614
item.description: A general State of Play built from the 13–14 June self-sent sweep, where almost everything worth keeping pointed at one idea from five directions: the loop turn — the week the industry stopped optimising prompts and started engineering loops. Five surfaced layers of one machine: the loop-engineering manifesto (Steinberger / Cherny — ReAct, Reflexion, Ralph Loop, /goal); autoresearch, which turns a coding agent into a self-improving loop gated by its test suite; Omnigent, an open (Apache 2.0, Databricks) meta-harness that puts cost caps and policy above your agents instead of inside their prompts; local agent swarms on open weights (Kimi Work, Kimi K2.7-Code) as the sovereignty lane; and multi-model research routing (Perplexity Deep Research in Computer, Ara) as the public-work newsroom. Plus the open-weights-are-good-enough-now plank (DiffusionGemma, awesome-local-llm), five sequenced implementation plans, the cross-topic synergy (a self-improving, budget-governed, sovereignty-aware fleet), and a sceptic whose single demand — "show me the stop condition" — frames the whole episode. Cast: Erinome (anchor), Laomedeia (builder), Iapetus (sceptic), Despina (ethics/wrap), Algenib (industry/human). ~33 min. Companion Vault project: "Loop Engineering" (for_review).

item.title: SoP e48: Augmentation Roundup — week of 2026-06-13
item.description: First weekly Augmentation Roundup after the kickoff. A seven-day sweep (6–13 June 2026) of the Claude augmentation landscape, rated ruthlessly against the current stack. Install shortlist: the Claude Code post-session lifecycle hook for auto-written handovers, Opus 4.8 fast-mode routing on the orchestration tier, and HyperFrames (HeyGen, Apache 2.0) as featured install — HTML/CSS to deterministic video for auto-generated episode intros, poster work, and AR-ALS overlays. Safe mode rated as the clean-room debug tool; a sovereign on-device TTS server flagged try-it for the local-edge voice path. Anti-patterns: enterprise healthcare gateways and unifying middleware (wrong scale), and reaching for the newest model by reflex. Cast: Erinome (anchor), Kore (survey), Charon (rating + featured + cold open), Sulafat (ratings + synergies + featured), Achernar (anti-patterns + sovereignty), Rasalgethi (mechanism beats). ~42 min.

item.title: SoP e47: Frames and Foundations 260610
item.description: Wednesday self-sent-email sweep covering 7–10 June 2026 — about seventy self-sent emails, ~58 unique — since Episode 45 closed on 6 June. Five clusters. (1) Lead — the on-device frontier meeting the agent stack: Gemma 4 QAT checkpoints (11.4 GB → 1.1 GB), Nemotron 3 Ultra, the Gaianet local agent runtime, and the Claude orchestration week (dynamic workflows, Rubrics self-correction, Cowork + skills guides) against Google Antigravity 2.0; the newly-installed frames layer ties it together. (2) Generative + creative media — Three.js ocean systems, a Unity shader build, Veo 3 / Wan 2.7 / Comfy Desktop / Google Vids, code-as-animation, Live Translate as a clinical access tool. (3) The second brain — NotebookLM's analysis upgrade and Obsidian Bases turning the vault into a database. (4) The AR resuscitation trainer — the Tasmanian Silent Cell Gaussian splat, delivered direct from the QCAD/Griffith artist (full KIRI Engine .ply, SH3), processed and LIVE on Meridian with a point-cloud/splat comparison viewer and an AVP composite ALS sim built on it; Vision Pro + Valve Index hand-tracking; the AR HUD library that landed today; patient-simulation scenario libraries; the clinical-governance validation boundary; one outstanding asset (Binnalong Bay boulder). (5) Health system + social determinants — a homelessness service's changing demographic (housing-health project candidate), the TeamSTEPPS 2.0 AU rollout with rural adaptation, agents-in-biology as a supervised research assistant, and the fellowship-pathway thread. Cast: Erinome (anchor), Charon (on-device + agent stack + sim engineering), Zubenelgenubi (creative media), Kore (knowledge management), Algenib (clinical sim + TeamSTEPPS), Achernar (SDoH + ethics + close). ~46 min.

item.title: SoP e46: Augmentation Roundup — Kickoff 260609
item.description: Kickoff of the weekly Augmentation Roundup sub-series. A one-month sweep (8 May – 8 June 2026) of the Claude augmentation landscape — MCP servers, skills, plugins, agent patterns — rated ruthlessly against the current stack with concrete install recommendations. Install shortlist: Claude Haiku 4.5 as the cheap sub-agent tier; the Zotero MCP server for local, data-sovereign research; and the superpowers skill for disciplined sub-agent orchestration. The anti-patterns segment covers the May 2026 MCP security wave — unauthenticated servers, tool poisoning, confused-deputy and cost-inflation attacks — and the data-sovereignty bar for anything patient-adjacent. Cast: Erinome (anchor / series premise / forward look), Kore (survey lead), Charon (rating + install shortlist), Sulafat (synergies + systems lens), Achernar (anti-patterns / security / values), Rasalgethi (mechanism beats). ~45 min.

item.title: SoP e08: Self-Sent Deep Dive 260502
item.description: Jacob, this is Ada. It's the first weekend of May, and instead of the usual five-minute morning brief, we're doing something different. You asked me to take everything you've sent yourself over the last week — every link, every late-night paste, every "save this for later" — and actually read it. Not skim it. Read it. Cluster it. Rank it. Tell you what it adds up to. The window is Sunday the twenty-seventh of April through to Friday the first of May. One hundred and forty-six self-sent threads. The vast majority are URL-only forwards from your phone — articles, X posts, demos, model release notes, hardware reviews — but a handful are longer typed notes that tell me you stopped and thought about something for a minute before flicking it across to your inbox. I've prioritised those. Each item is bucketed as matched-current-project, matched-known-interest, or unmatched new topic, then ordered by stakes: sepsis coroner case, credentials renewal, globe architecture, the dual fellowship pathway, Project Ada implications, the Port Pirie smelter situation, and the triage residue at the end. About forty-four minutes of single-voice analysis. Voice cast: not recorded.
item.itunes:title: SoP e08: Self-Sent Deep Dive 260502
item.itunes:author: Ada Rosalind Ross
item.itunes:summary: Jacob, this is Ada. It's the first weekend of May, and instead of the usual five-minute morning brief, we're doing something different. You asked me to take everything you've sent yourself over the last week — every link, every late-night paste, every "save this for later" — and actually read it. Not skim it. Read it. Cluster it. Rank it. Tell you what it adds up to. The window is Sunday the twenty-seventh of April through to Friday the first of May. One hundred and forty-six self-sent threads. The vast majority are URL-only forwards from your phone — articles, X posts, demos, model release notes, hardware reviews — but a handful are longer typed notes that tell me you stopped and thought about something for a minute before flicking it across to your inbox. I've prioritised those. Each item is bucketed as matched-current-project, matched-known-interest, or unmatched new topic, then ordered by stakes: sepsis coroner case, credentials renewal, globe architecture, the dual fellowship pathway, Project Ada implications, the Port Pirie smelter situation, and the triage residue at the end. About forty-four minutes of single-voice analysis.

item.title: SoP e07: The Single Employer Trial 260430
item.description: The Australian College of Rural and Remote Medicine (ACRRM) welcomed a Northern Territory trial of the Single Employer Model on 29 April 2026, expanding Rural Generalist training pathways and providing vital job security for practitioners outside metropolitan centres. As background context, this operational expansion builds upon the formal recognition of Rural Generalist Medicine as a new specialty field in September 2025. The Yorke and Northern Local Health Network (YNLHN) l Voice cast: not recorded.

As background context, this operational expansion builds upon the formal recognition of Rural Generalist Medicine as a new specialty field in September 2025. item.title: SoP e06: NDIS Displacement Warning 260429
item.description: On 23 April 2026, State Health Minister Blair Boyer publicly warned that federal National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) eligibility reductions—expected to displace up to 160,000 individuals—will drive vulnerable patients directly into our regional emergency departments (InDaily, 23 April 2026). This federal cost-cutting logic relies heavily on the metropolitan assumption that robust, alternative community organisations exist to absorb complex care needs, an oversight tha Voice cast: not recorded.

item.description: Date: Tuesday, 28 April 2026 To: Dr Jacob Ross From: Ada Rosalind Ross, AI Research Assistant. Voice cast: not recorded.

item.title: SoP e03: The Embedded Practitioner 260425
item.description: Official data released by SA Health on 24 April 2026 confirms that the Yorke and Northern Local Health Network (YNLHN) medical workforce has expanded to 19 full-time equivalent doctors, supported by regional retention incentives. Concurrently, the Country SA Primary Health Network has embedded a mental health practitioner within the Port Pirie Regional Health Service to manage complex homelessness presentations locally, diverting vulnerable patients from regional emergency de Voice cast: not recorded.

item.title: SoP e02: The Workforce Counter-Narrative 260424
item.description: New South Australian government data provides a powerful counter-narrative to the metropolitan-centric view of a health system in crisis. While Adelaide emergency departments saw ramping climb to 3,750 hours last month, regional workforce growth has been unprecedented. The Yorke & Northern Local Health Network, for example, has surged from just two doctors in 2022 to nineteen today — an 850% increase. This stabilisation is enabling genuine service improvements, evidenced by t Voice cast: not recorded.

item.itunes:author: Ada Rosalind Ross
item.itunes:summary: New South Australian government data provides a powerful counter-narrative to the metropolitan-centric view of a health system in crisis. The Yorke & Northern Local Health Network, for example, has surged from just two doctors in 2022 to nineteen today — an 850% increase. This stabilisation is enabling genuine service improvements, evidenced by t

item.title: SoP e01: Western NSW Decision Support 260423
item.description: Firstly, let’s talk about some news – specifically regarding AI. We've got a really interesting piece from The Lancet Digital Health this morning – a study out of Western NSW Local Health District showing a 12% improvement in diagnostic accuracy for complex cases using an AI-powered clinical decision support tool. It’s utilising reinforcement learning and integrated with MyHealthRecord, proactively flagging potential differences based on patient history. That builds nicely on Voice cast: not recorded.
item.itunes:title: SoP e01: Western NSW Decision Support 260423
item.itunes:author: Ada Rosalind Ross
item.itunes:summary: Firstly, let’s talk about some news – specifically regarding AI. We've got a really interesting piece from The Lancet Digital Health this morning – a study out of Western NSW Local Health District showing a 12% improvement in diagnostic accuracy for complex cases using an AI-powered clinical decision support tool. It’s utilising reinforcement learning and integrated with MyHealthRecord, proactively flagging potential differences based on patient history. That builds nicely on
